# Brief: Dana (PERSONA-009)
## Upcoming Meeting: 2026-04-07

## Since Last Meeting

No new evidence entered the record between April 2 and April 7. The gap is five days across a holiday-adjacent weekend; nothing unusual about that timeline.

What matters for tonight is the state you walk in with. The school board declined to vote on April 2 — the second consecutive meeting it has adjourned without a budget. The $300,000 in additional state funding announced live mid-meeting was unconfirmed as of adjournment, and the separate $750,000 EPS formula signal remains a signal, not a number. Neither the board chair nor the superintendent put a confirmed figure on the record.

Tonight's city council meeting is the deadline the city manager named as the forcing function. That deadline is now here, and the school board still has no budget. Watch whether the council treats that as a procedural footnote or a substantive confrontation.

---

## Open Questions

Carrying into tonight:

- **Is the $300,000 state funding real yet?** The mid-meeting texts on April 2 moved the board to defer — but if that money isn't confirmed in writing before tonight, the school board bought time on a rumor. Did the district get anything in writing from Augusta over the past five days?
- **What actually happens at tonight's deadline?** The city manager said April 7 was the school budget submission deadline. The school board hasn't passed a budget. Does the council formally acknowledge that? Does anyone go on record about consequences?
- **Is the school budget presentation on tonight's agenda actually presentable?** The budget timeline lists April 7 as "Presentation and Public Hearing on Budget" with school included — but there's no adopted school budget. What does the city present, and who answers questions about it?
- **Who shows up in public comment?** The community mobilization at April 2 was the loudest Dana has tracked all season — parents naming board member election years, announcing referendum no-votes as pressure. Does that crowd follow the story to City Hall tonight?
- **Can Dana get Mr. Wetzel — or anyone from the 72 — on camera before the budget story resolves one way or another?** The human face of the RIF notices is still unfilmed.
- **Does the Suncor license renewal item tonight give the Toss the Tanks campaign an actual news hook, or is it another procedural step?** Nine months of city invitation, nine months of no Suncor response — does tonight's DEP comment letter authorization change that?
- **Is the Holman electoral accountability story starting to move?** Brian Green named board member term expiration years publicly on April 2. Does that thread have any presence at tonight's council meeting?
- **What is the postponed item (H.1) that got kicked from March 3 and March 19?** Two postponements means something was contested enough to delay. Worth knowing before the meeting starts.

---

## Agenda Implications

**Section D — Board and Committee Vacancies (Items 10–16)**

Seven advisory board positions in some stage of vacancy or anticipated turnover. Three seats (items 13–15) have incumbents who haven't confirmed reappointment — terms ending May 4. This is background civic housekeeping, not a story, unless one of those seats connects to a body with budget or development jurisdiction.

*What to watch for:* If any of the three unconfirmed incumbents sits on the Planning Board or a body touching school facilities or city finances, that could fold into the electoral accountability thread. Otherwise, skip.

---

**Section E — Consent Agenda (Items E.1–E.11)**

Routine approvals including a speed enforcement grant (no local match, runs through September 2026), a Maine Office of Community Affairs grant (application and award letter attached but no dollar figure in the agenda text), a pool deck flooring bid ($40,228 to Epoxy PROS of New England), and a Shoreway Trail agreement between the city and SMCC.

*What to watch for:* Whether any consent item gets pulled for separate discussion. The Maine Office of Community Affairs grant (E.9) could be social services-related — worth checking the attached award letter if you can get it. None of these appear to be primary story material on their own.

---

**Section F — Public Comment**

This is your primary hunting ground tonight. The community mobilization that emerged at April 2 — parents naming board member term expiration years, announcing referendum no-votes — followed the school board meeting. Tonight's council meeting is the April 7 deadline those same parents know about.

*What to listen for:* Whether the parents who showed up at April 2 redirect to City Hall tonight. Whether anyone puts the school board's second consecutive no-vote explicitly on the council's record. Whether anyone names the $300,000 state funding claim and asks whether it's been verified. Whether seniors or fixed-income homeowners speak to the layered tax burden — school referendum plus public safety bond plus sewer rates — in a single statement.

*Soundbyte to watch for:* Anyone who can articulate the combinatorial burden in plain language in under 20 seconds. That's the segment lede you've been assembling since January.

---

**Section G.2 — City Budget Presentation and Public Hearing**

This is the most consequential item on the agenda for your coverage. The city manager's budget timeline, embedded in the position paper, shows:

- April 7: Presentation and Public Hearing on Budget
- April 14: Budget Workshop #1 — **School is listed first**
- May 5: Public Hearing/Approval of School Budget to Send to Voters
- June 9: School Budget Referendum

The school board has not passed a budget. That means the city is attempting to hold a public hearing on a budget that doesn't yet exist in its school component. This is either a procedural formality — the city presents what it has and notes the school submission is pending — or it becomes a live confrontation about what happens next.

*What to listen for:* How the city manager characterizes the school board's missing submission. Whether any council member asks what the consequence is of a school budget arriving after the April 7 deadline. Whether the May 5 "Public Hearing/Approval" date is described as firm or contingent. Whether the 18.95% school tax figure appears anywhere in the city's presentation, and what framing surrounds it.

*Questions to prepare:* "What does the city present tonight if the school board hasn't adopted a budget?" "Is the May 5 deadline still operative if the school board doesn't act before then?" "What happens to the June 9 referendum if the board misses May 5?"

This is the meeting where the city's budget calendar and the school board's dysfunction collide on the public record. If the city manager has to explain the gap on camera, that's usable footage.

**Section H.1 — Postponed Item (Two Prior Delays, March 3 and March 19)**

This item was held back twice. The materials reference an "Alternative Order" with red-line revisions to an original order from last year, plus a March 3 order the city manager is no longer recommending. The agenda doesn't name the subject.

*What to listen for:* What the item actually is. Two postponements with a complete rewrite of the proposed order suggests contested territory. If it touches housing, development, or any of the city's fiscal obligations, it belongs in your open threads. If it's land use or a service contract, probably not.

**Sections H.2–H.4 — E-Bike Ordinances**

Three ordinance chapters being amended to address e-bikes, with the Parks & Recreation Director present. Not your beat tonight.

---

**Section H.8 — Suncor/PPLC License Renewal Comment Letter**

The council is being asked to authorize submission of a comment letter to Maine DEP on Portland Pipe Line Corporation's license renewal. The position paper itemizes what the letter addresses: spill prevention, closure planning and cost estimates, financial assurance, inactive tanks, site conditions, natural hazard risk, and future transfer of ownership.

This is the Toss the Tanks story moving. The city has been on record since at least September 2025 inviting Suncor to discuss the tank farm's future — nine months with no confirmed response. Tonight, the city shifts from invitation to formal regulatory comment on the license renewal itself.

*What to listen for:* Whether the city manager or any council member references Suncor's response (or continued non-response) to the September 2025 invitation. Whether the language in the comment letter is defensive (compliance-focused) or assertive (closure-oriented). Whether any council member goes on record about the financial assurance or natural hazard risk language — those are camera-ready phrases for a climate and infrastructure story.

*Why this matters for your production:* The tank farm sits on the South Portland waterfront. It's visible b-roll. If the council is formally asking DEP to address closure planning and financial liability in the license renewal, that's a stronger news hook than the advocacy campaign has had all season. Check whether any Toss the Tanks organizer is attending or speaking in public comment.

---

**Section I.1 — City Manager Report**

Position paper attached but contents not listed in the agenda text. This is where updates that don't fit elsewhere land — including, potentially, a status note on the school budget situation.

*What to listen for:* Whether the city manager voluntarily addresses the school board's April 7 deadline and what submission, if any, was received. Whether the $300,000 state funding announcement from April 2 gets any mention or characterization.

---

## Watch For

- **Whether the city formally acknowledges the school board has no budget to present.** If the city manager has to say on the record that the school budget presentation is pending because the board hasn't voted, that's a clip. If a council member asks what happens next under those conditions, that's a clip with a follow-up question attached.

- **Public comment as the school budget's de facto continuation.** The April 2 community shift from passive to active — parents naming election years — may show up at City Hall tonight. Watch for the same speakers or organized groups redirecting pressure from the board to the council.

- **The Suncor comment letter vote.** If council members characterize the financial assurance or closure planning language on camera, that's the strongest Toss the Tanks hook you've had. It's a vote, not just a statement — that's a news verb.

- **What the May 5 deadline language sounds like.** The budget timeline shows May 5 as the date the school budget goes to voters. If a council member or the city manager says anything that qualifies that date — "assuming the board acts," "if we receive a submission" — that changes the referendum countdown story and is worth flagging for your anchor.

- **Whether any council member connects the school budget crisis to the city's $28.8 million fund balance.** That fiscal asymmetry — the city sitting on a clean audit and healthy reserves while the school district has a material weakness and a missed deadline — has never been put by a council member on camera in the same breath. If someone does that tonight, it's your segment spine.

- **The postponed H.1 item.** Find out what it is before the meeting starts if you can. Two postponements plus a complete rewrite of the proposed order means there was disagreement about something. If it touches housing, immigration, or fiscal obligations, it may be a buried story.

- **Tone from the city manager when the school budget comes up.** He characterized the February guidance process as structurally "unfair" to the school board. Watch whether that framing has shifted now that the board has missed its own deadline, not just the council's guidance. Tone shift from neutral to impatient — or the reverse — is a signal.

- **Whether Councilor Matthews speaks.** He was the lone no vote on March 19 and has been the clearest on-record voice for the combinatorial tax burden. If he connects tonight's budget presentation, the school board's missing submission, and the November public safety bond in a single statement, that's the closest you've come to the segment you've been building since January.

- **Camera-ready moment assessment:** The April 7 meeting is worth sending a crew to on the strength of one factor alone — the school budget deadline colliding with a city budget presentation in the same room on the same night. If the city manager has to publicly describe what the city does when the school board hasn't met its submission deadline, that's news. Send the crew.
